explanation_md: `std::cerr` writes to standard error, which is a separate stream from `std::cout`'s standard output. The three `std::cout` writes produce `abd` on stdout; `c` appears on stderr. The question asks only about stdout.

prompt_md: Why is `std::cerr` unbuffered while `std::cout` is buffered by default?

options.a: Because cerr is older than cout and the old design didn't support buffering.
options.b: So that error and diagnostic messages appear immediately, even if the program crashes before the buffer would be flushed.
options.c: Because cerr can only hold one character at a time.
options.d: Because cerr is connected to a different file descriptor that the OS doesn't buffer.

explanation_md: The purpose of `std::cerr` is to deliver messages even under abnormal program termination. Buffered output might be lost if the program crashes before the buffer is flushed. `std::cout` is buffered for performance — fewer OS calls — because losing a few lines of normal output on a crash is acceptable; losing an error message is not.

prompt_md: Which of the following correctly describes the relationship between `std::istream` and `std::cin`?

options.a: `std::cin` is a type; `std::istream` is an instance of that type.
options.b: `std::istream` is a class; `std::cin` is a global object of type `std::istream`.
options.c: `std::istream` and `std::cin` are both type aliases for the same built-in type.
options.d: `std::cin` is a macro that expands to `std::istream::get()`.

explanation_md: `std::istream` is a class defined in `<iostream>`. `std::cin` is a pre-defined global object of that class (technically `std::basic_istream<char>`) that the runtime connects to the program's standard input. You can pass `std::cin` wherever an `std::istream&` is expected.
`std::cout` is an `std::ostream`, so it can be passed as an `std::ostream&`. Each call writes the integer followed by a newline, producing two lines: `7` then `3`.
